# Benchmark Models


<!-- WARNING: THIS FILE WAS AUTOGENERATED! DO NOT EDIT! -->

We will first open the data

``` python
DATA_PATH = Path("../testing_data")

df = pd.read_csv(
    DATA_PATH/'hydro_example.csv', 
    usecols=['time', 'smoothed_rain', 'Q_mgb', 'Q_obs'], 
    index_col='time',
    converters={"time": pd.to_datetime}
    )
```

We can now take a quick look to the data to see the trends and the
expected improvement

``` python
def normalize(df):
    return (df - df.min()) / (df.max() - df.min())

normalize(df).plot()#.hvplot.line()
```

## Data preprocessing

Select feature and target columns

``` python
x_col, y_col = ['smoothed_rain','Q_mgb'], ['Q_obs']
```

Split data

``` python
train_mask = df.index < '2019-01-01'
train = df[train_mask]
valid = df[~train_mask]
```

Scale data

``` python
features_scaler = RobustScaler()
train[x_col] = features_scaler.fit_transform(train[x_col])
valid[x_col] = features_scaler.transform(valid[x_col])
```

## Feature generator

> Feature generator that uses a sliding window of n previous timesteps
> and polynomial features to predict the next value, enabling learning
> of temporal patterns and non-linear relationships

------------------------------------------------------------------------

<a
href="https://github.com/iraind/ombs_senegal/blob/main/ombs_senegal/benchmark_model.py#L16"
target="_blank" style="float:right; font-size:smaller">source</a>

### FeatureGenerator

>  FeatureGenerator (context_window:int=10, target_window:int=10,
>                        degree:int=1)

*Transforms time series data into feature matrices suitable for machine
learning models. Creates lagged features using a sliding window and
optionally generates polynomial features to capture non-linear
relationships between variables.*

``` python
feature_generator = FeatureGenerator(context_window=1, target_window=2, degree=2)
train_x, train_y = feature_generator.generate(train, x_col, y_col)
train_x.head(3), train_y.head(3)
```

### Benchmark model

We chose a linear regression model as our benchmark because: 1. When
combined with our feature generator, it provides a simple yet effective
baseline 2. The polynomial features allow it to capture non-linear
relationships in the data 3. The sliding window enables learning of
temporal patterns across multiple timesteps 4. Linear regression models
are highly interpretable and computationally efficient

------------------------------------------------------------------------

<a
href="https://github.com/iraind/ombs_senegal/blob/main/ombs_senegal/benchmark_model.py#L63"
target="_blank" style="float:right; font-size:smaller">source</a>

### SimpleRegressionModel

>  SimpleRegressionModel ()

*Initialize self. See help(type(self)) for accurate signature.*

## Model training and comparison

We can now make a simple prediction to compare different window sizes
and differen polynomial degrees

``` python
predictions = []
scores = []
for degree in range(1, 2+1):
    for window in range(10, 20+1, 10):
        feature_generator = FeatureGenerator(context_window=window, target_window=4, degree=degree)        
        train_x, train_y = feature_generator.generate(train, x_col, y_col)
        valid_x, valid_y = feature_generator.generate(valid, x_col, y_col)

        model = SimpleRegressionModel()
        model.fit(train_x, train_y)
        
        pred = model.predict_as_dataframe(valid_x, degree, window)
        predictions.append(pred)

predictions = pd.concat(predictions).reorder_levels(['degree', 'ctx_window', 'time']).to_xarray()
observations = valid[y_col].to_xarray().sel(time=slice(predictions.time.min(), predictions.time.max()))
```

## Scoring

Since we have generated multiple predictions with different parameters,
we will select only the best performing models according to each metric

------------------------------------------------------------------------

<a
href="https://github.com/iraind/ombs_senegal/blob/main/ombs_senegal/benchmark_model.py#L83"
target="_blank" style="float:right; font-size:smaller">source</a>

### BenchmarkScores

>  BenchmarkScores ()

\*A class to compute and compare different error metrics between
predictions and observations.

This class provides a flexible way to compute multiple error metrics
between predicted and observed values. New metrics can be easily added
by implementing them as methods. The new metrics can then be used by
passing their method names as strings to compute_scores().

Example:

``` python
class CustomBenchmarkScores(BenchmarkScores):
    def mse(self, pred, obs):
        error = ((pred - obs)**2).mean(dim="time") 
        error.name = "mse"
        return error

# Can then be used as:
scores = compute_scores(pred, obs, metrics=["mae", "rmse", "mse"])
```*


::: {.cell}
``` {.python .cell-code}
benchmark_scores = BenchmarkScores()
scores_ds = benchmark_scores.compute_scores(
    predictions,
    observations["Q_obs"],
    ["mae", "rmse", "nse", "kge"])
best_scores = benchmark_scores.find_nbest_scores(
    scores_ds,
    how={"mae": "min", "rmse": "min", "nse": "max", "kge": "max"},
    n=1)
```

:::

plot_interactive_benchmark_scores(best_scores,)
```

## Time series verification

Now that we have identified the optimal model parameters, let’s verify
its performance by comparing the predicted discharge values with both
observed values and MGB model predictions. This comparison will be done
across the full prediction horizon to assess how well our model
maintains its predictive power over time. We’ll visualize these
comparisons using time series plots that show the observed discharge,
our model’s predictions, and the MGB model predictions side by side.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

<a
href="https://github.com/iraind/ombs_senegal/blob/main/ombs_senegal/benchmark_model.py#L296"
target="_blank" style="float:right; font-size:smaller">source</a>

### plot_prediction_comparison

>  plot_prediction_comparison (observed:xarray.core.dataarray.DataArray,
>                                  predicted:xarray.core.dataset.Dataset,
>                                  mgb:xarray.core.dataarray.DataArray,
>                                  best_model:dict=None,
>                                  scores:xarray.core.dataset.Dataset=None)

*Plot comparison between observed, predicted and MGB discharge values
for a i-day horizon.*

<table>
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 6%" />
<col style="width: 25%" />
<col style="width: 34%" />
<col style="width: 34%" />
</colgroup>
<thead>
<tr>
<th></th>

<th><strong>Details</strong></th>
</tr>
</thead>

horizon (t+i)</td>

window as value</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Returns</strong></td>
<td><strong>Figure</strong></td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

From the previous analysis we can decides which model works the best for
each predicted time step. We will use the best model for each time step
to plot the prediction comparison. As example:

``` python
best_models={
    "t+1": {"degree": 2, "ctx_window": 20}, 
    "t+2": {"degree": 2, "ctx_window": 20}, 
    "t+3": {"degree": 2, "ctx_window": 10},
    "t+4": {"degree": 2, "ctx_window": 10},
    }
fig = plot_prediction_comparison(
    observed=observations["Q_obs"], 
    predicted=predictions, 
    best_model=best_models, 
    mgb=df[~train_mask]["Q_mgb"].to_xarray(),
    scores=scores_ds
    )
plt.show()
```

## Save prediction data

We save the best model for later use
